title: Homeostatic Preferences: Stay in the Band

runtime.expected_outcome: C with a band of high-value observations (e.g. any goal-adjacent tile) lets the agent settle in the band without requiring a specific goal tile.

math.latex: \log p(o) \text{ peaked over a set } O^* \subset O

audiences.kid: Instead of one favourite spot, the agent has a whole comfy zone. It just has to stay inside.
audiences.real: Homeostasis emerges naturally from a C vector with a multi-observation peak. No separate control loop needed.
audiences.equation: Preference peaks over a set O* -> pragmatic term rewards policies that keep q(o|pi) supported on O*.
audiences.derivation: Active inference subsumes homeostasis as a special case of surprise minimisation: the agent's preferences are just the vital set (Friston 2013). The C vector encodes the band.

orchestrate.objective: Show that a multi-peaked C produces homeostatic behaviour without extra machinery.
orchestrate.context: corridor_turns; c_preference_override assigns high logits to all tiles within 2 steps of the goal.

credits: Parr, Pezzulo & Friston (2022), Active Inference, MIT Press (CC BY-NC-ND), Ch 4.
Friston (2013), Life as we know it.
